metadata.visit_title: Research screening visit — anti-obesity medication study

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Maria. It's really nice to meet you. Come on in and have a seat. How are you doing today?

PT: Good morning, Doctor. I'm doing pretty well, thank you. A little nervous, honestly — I've never been part of a research study before.

DR: That's completely normal, and I'm glad you asked to come in. There's no pressure at all today. This is just a screening visit — we're going to talk about how you've been doing, especially since your surgery, and I'll walk you through what the study involves. Then you can decide if it feels right for you. Sound okay?

PT: Yes, that sounds good.

DR: Great. So let me start with the basics. My notes show you had metabolic surgery — the bariatric procedure — about six, maybe seven months ago now. Does that sound right?

PT: Yes, it was back in late July. So about seven months.

DR: Perfect. And how has that whole journey been for you?

PT: It's been a big adjustment, but a good one. The first couple of months were the hardest — learning how to eat again, really. Small portions, chewing everything so carefully. But now I feel like I've got a rhythm.

DR: That's wonderful to hear. The first few months really are the steepest learning curve. Can you tell me about your weight since the surgery? Any sense of the trend?

PT: I've lost quite a bit. I don't remember the exact number, but I've gone down a couple of clothing sizes. It's been steady, not all at once.

DR: Steady is exactly what we like to see. Your BMI today is around 32, which tells me you've made real progress but you're still in a range where continued support can help. Have you noticed the weight loss slowing down at all recently?

PT: Yes, actually. The first few months it just kept coming off, and now it feels like it's kind of plateaued the last few weeks.

DR: That's very common around this point — the body finds a new set point and things level off. That plateau is actually one of the reasons studies like this one exist. Let's talk about your eating. What does a typical day of meals look like for you now?

PT: I have a protein shake in the morning, usually. Then a small lunch — some chicken or fish, a few vegetables. Dinner is similar, just small. I try to eat protein first like they told me. I snack on Greek yogurt sometimes.

DR: That sounds like you've really taken the nutrition guidance to heart. Are you keeping up with your vitamins and any supplements the surgical team recommended?

PT: Yes, I take a multivitamin and a chewable calcium. And a B12 one.

DR: Excellent. That's important — I'm glad you're staying on top of it. Any trouble with foods not sitting well, nausea, vomiting, anything like that?

PT: Early on if I ate too fast I'd feel awful. Now I know my limits. Occasionally something too sweet bothers me, so I just avoid that.

DR: That's a good instinct. And how about your energy levels day to day?

PT: Much better than before surgery, honestly. I used to feel exhausted all the time. Now I actually have energy in the afternoons.

DR: That's one of the best things people report. What about activity — are you moving more?

PT: I've started walking every day, about thirty minutes. My daughter and I go together in the evenings. I've been thinking about trying a swim class too.

DR: I love that. Walking daily and building it into your routine with family — that's the kind of habit that lasts. A swim class would be a great addition, gentle on the joints. Any joint pain or other physical concerns?

PT: Not really. My knees used to ache and that's much better now with the weight off.

DR: Good. Let me ask a few health-history questions. Are you taking any medications currently?

PT: No, nothing regular. Just the vitamins.

DR: Any other medical conditions we should know about — diabetes, blood pressure, thyroid, anything?

PT: No, thankfully. My doctor said my numbers all looked good at my last check.

DR: That's great to hear, and it fits with what I'm seeing in your chart — things look really clean. Now let me tell you about the study. It's evaluating an anti-obesity medication designed to support continued, healthy weight loss in people who've had metabolic surgery, especially when the loss starts to plateau like yours has.

PT: So it would help me keep going past this plateau?

DR: That's the idea we're testing. If you enroll, you'd be assigned to either the study medication or a placebo — that's an inactive comparison — and neither of us would know which you're receiving. You'd come in for regular visits so we can monitor your weight, your labs, and how you're feeling. Everything is closely supervised.

PT: How often would I have to come in?

DR: Roughly once a month at first, then spacing out. Each visit is about an hour. You'd keep up your current eating and activity habits — nothing about your routine has to change dramatically. And you can withdraw at any time, no questions asked.

PT: That's reassuring. And it's free?

DR: Yes, all study-related visits, medication, and labs are covered, and there's compensation for your time. I'll give you the full consent form to read at home. Take your time with it — there's no rush to decide today.

PT: I appreciate that. I think I'm interested. I just want to read everything first.

DR: That's exactly the right approach. Today I'll record your vitals and BMI, and we'll do a basic screening check. Everything I've heard tells me you're doing beautifully after your surgery. Do you have any questions for me right now?

PT: Not right now. Maybe after I read the form.

DR: Perfect. Write them down as you think of them and we'll go through every one. I'm really glad you came in, Maria. You should feel proud of the work you've put in.

PT: Thank you, Doctor. That means a lot.
note:
**Subjective:**
Ms. Maria Elena Okonkwo is a 42-year-old woman presenting for a research screening visit for an anti-obesity medication study, approximately 6-7 months following metabolic (bariatric) surgery (performed late July 2025). She reports doing well overall. The initial post-operative months required significant adjustment to eating patterns, but she now feels she has established a stable routine. She describes steady weight loss since surgery, down approximately two clothing sizes, with a plateau noted over the past few weeks.

Diet: Protein-forward, small portions — morning protein shake, small lunch (chicken or fish with vegetables), similar dinner, Greek yogurt as a snack. She adheres to "protein first" guidance and is compliant with recommended supplements (multivitamin, chewable calcium, B12). Early post-op nausea with rapid eating has resolved; she avoids overly sweet foods that cause discomfort. No current vomiting or intolerance concerns.

Activity/Energy: Markedly improved energy compared to before surgery. Walks ~30 minutes daily with her daughter and is considering a swim class. Prior knee pain has improved with weight loss.

No current medications aside from vitamins. Denies diabetes, hypertension, thyroid, or other chronic conditions; reports normal labs at last check.

**Objective:**
BMI: 32.0 kg/m2.
Patient appears well, alert, and engaged; no acute distress. Post-bariatric surgical history confirmed. Chart reviewed — no exclusionary conditions identified. No current prescription medications.

**Assessment:**
42-year-old woman approximately 6-7 months post metabolic/bariatric surgery, currently with a BMI of 32.0 kg/m2 (obesity, active). She is progressing well post-operatively with good dietary adherence, supplement compliance, improved energy, and increasing physical activity. Weight loss has plateaued, which is developmentally appropriate at this stage. She presents as a suitable candidate for the anti-obesity medication study; chart is unremarkable for exclusions.

1. Obesity, BMI 32.0 — status post bariatric surgery, weight loss plateau.
2. History of bariatric surgery — recovering well, tolerating diet, adherent to supplements.
3. Research study screening — candidate appears eligible pending completion of consent and screening evaluations.

**Plan:**
1. Provided detailed explanation of the anti-obesity medication study, including randomization to study drug vs. placebo, double-blind design, visit schedule (approximately monthly initially), monitoring procedures, no-cost study medication/labs/visits, compensation, and the right to withdraw at any time.
2. Provided informed consent document for the patient to review at home; no decision required today. Encouraged her to record any questions.
3. Recorded vitals and BMI; baseline screening evaluation performed today.
4. Continue current diet, activity, and supplement regimen.
5. Reinforced positive habits — daily walking, protein-first eating, hydration, supplement adherence. Supported plan to add swimming.
6. Follow-up to complete consent and remaining screening steps if the patient elects to proceed.
after_visit_summary:
Hi Maria,

Thank you for coming in today for your research study screening visit. It was great to hear how well you're doing about seven months after your surgery!

How you're doing:
- Your weight loss has been steady, and it's normal for it to level off (plateau) around this stage.
- Your eating habits look great — protein first, small balanced meals, and staying on your vitamins (multivitamin, calcium, B12). Keep it up!
- Your energy is much better, and your daily 30-minute walks are excellent. A swim class would be a wonderful addition.
- Today your BMI was 32.

About the study:
- The study is testing a medication to support continued healthy weight loss after bariatric surgery.
- If you join, you'd be randomly assigned to either the study medication or a placebo, and you'd come in for regular check-ins (about monthly at first).
- Study visits, medication, and labs are provided at no cost, and there is compensation for your time.
- Joining is completely your choice, and you can stop at any time.

Next steps:
- Read the consent form at home — there's no rush.
- Write down any questions and we'll go over each one together.
- Contact the study team when you're ready or if you have questions.

You should be proud of your progress. We look forward to hearing from you!

metadata.visit_title: Research screening visit — anti-obesity medication study

transcript:
DR: Good morning, David. Come on in, have a seat. I'm Dr. Reyes — I'll be doing your screening visit today for the research study. How are you doing?

PT: Morning, doctor. I'm doing all right, thanks. A little curious about all this, honestly.

DR: That's completely fair. Let me start by explaining what today is. This is a screening visit for a clinical trial looking at a new anti-obesity medication. Screening just means we check whether you're a good fit for the study — I'll ask about your health, how you've been since your surgery, review some of your history, and go over what the study would involve. Nothing gets decided today, and you're free to ask anything.

PT: Okay, that sounds good.

DR: So let's start with the surgery. My notes say you had bariatric surgery — that was about six or seven months ago?

PT: Yeah, back at the end of last summer. So we're coming up on, what, seven months now.

DR: How has recovery been overall?

PT: Honestly, better than I expected. The first few weeks were rough — getting used to eating so little, figuring out what my stomach could tolerate. But now I feel like I've kind of found my rhythm. No major complications, no hospital trips since the surgery itself.

DR: That's great to hear. Let's talk about weight. Have you been tracking it?

PT: Pretty closely, yeah. I was up around 250 before surgery. Last I checked at home I was around 233, 234. So it's coming down, just slower than I hoped the last month or so.

DR: A plateau around this point is very common, so don't be discouraged. We did check your BMI here today and it's about 33.4. That does still put you in the obesity range, which — I'll be honest — is actually relevant to whether you'd qualify for this particular study.

PT: Right, that makes sense. That's kind of why I'm frustrated. I did the big thing, the surgery, and I'm still technically obese on paper.

DR: And I want to reframe that a little for you. You've lost a meaningful amount of weight and you're clearly making changes. A number on a chart doesn't erase that. Tell me about your eating now — what does a typical day look like?

PT: Small meals. I do a protein shake in the morning usually, because solid food first thing still doesn't sit great. Lunch is something like eggs or chicken and a few vegetables. Dinner's small too. I have to eat slowly or I feel it.

DR: Are you meeting your protein goals? That's something the surgical team usually emphasizes.

PT: They told me to aim for like 60 to 80 grams. Some days I hit it, some days I fall short, especially if I'm busy at work and just grab something.

DR: That's honest, and pretty typical. Are you taking your vitamins — the multivitamin, B12, that kind of thing?

PT: Yeah, I'm pretty consistent with those. The one thing I sometimes forget is the calcium.

DR: Okay. We can talk more about that. How about activity — are you moving much?

PT: I've started walking. I do maybe 30 minutes most evenings, and on weekends I've been getting out longer, like an hour. I haven't gotten back to any real gym routine though.

DR: Walking is genuinely one of the best things you can do, so that counts for a lot. And your energy — how's that day to day?

PT: Way better than before surgery. I used to be exhausted all the time. Now I'd say I'm normal-tired, not dragging myself around. I sleep better too.

PT: I do get a little lightheaded sometimes if I stand up fast or if I haven't eaten.

DR: That's worth keeping an eye on — it can happen when you're eating smaller volumes and maybe not getting enough fluids. Are you drinking enough water?

PT: Probably not, if I'm honest. I sip through the day but I don't think I'm hitting the recommended amount.

DR: Let's put fluids on the list to work on. Any nausea, vomiting, trouble swallowing, or reflux?

PT: Occasional reflux at night if I eat too late. No vomiting. Swallowing's fine.

DR: Good. Any other concerns you wanted to bring up before I go over the study details?

PT: Mainly just — is it safe to be in a medication study after having surgery? I don't want to mess up what I've done.

DR: That's exactly the right question, and it's part of why screening exists. The study team carefully reviews your surgical history and current health before anyone is enrolled. Your recent surgery isn't automatically a problem, but it's one of the things we evaluate closely. If anything about your situation made participation unsafe, we would not proceed — your health comes first.

PT: That's reassuring.

DR: So here's the shape of the study: it's testing an anti-obesity medication against a comparison group. If you enrolled, there'd be regular visits — weight checks, some bloodwork, questionnaires about diet and how you're feeling. You'd be able to withdraw at any time for any reason. Today is just to see whether you meet the initial criteria and to answer your questions.

PT: How long does the whole thing run?

DR: The full study spans several months of active visits. I'll give you the detailed information sheet to take home so you're not making any decision on the spot. I really want you to read it and think it over.

PT: Okay. I appreciate that. I think I'm interested, I just want to understand it fully.

DR: Perfect attitude. Here's what I'll do next — I'll document today's assessment, we'll note your current BMI and how you're doing post-surgery, and I'll flag the items we discussed: protein intake, calcium, and hydration. Keep up the walking. If that lightheadedness gets worse or you have any new symptoms, I want you to reach out before your next visit.

PT: Will do. Thanks, Dr. Reyes.

DR: My pleasure, David. Take the paperwork, read it over, and we'll talk again. You're doing well — really.
note:
**Subjective:**
Mr. David Hartmann is a 45-year-old male presenting for a research-study screening visit for an anti-obesity medication trial. He is approximately 6–7 months status post bariatric surgery (performed late summer prior). He reports recovery has been better than expected, with an initial difficult adjustment period but no complications and no hospitalizations since surgery.

Weight: self-reports pre-operative weight ~250 lb, now ~233–234 lb, with perceived plateau over the past month. Diet consists of small meals — morning protein shake (solid food not well tolerated first thing), modest protein-based lunch and dinner, eating slowly. Protein goal is 60–80 g/day; he meets this inconsistently, particularly on busy workdays. Adherent to multivitamin and B12; inconsistent with calcium supplementation.

Activity: walking ~30 minutes most evenings, up to an hour on weekends; no structured gym routine yet. Energy markedly improved compared to pre-surgery, sleeps better. Reports occasional lightheadedness with rapid standing or when not having eaten. Fluid intake likely below recommended. Occasional nighttime reflux with late meals; denies vomiting or dysphagia.

Patient asked about the safety of participating in a medication study after surgery and expressed cautious interest. No decision made today.

**Objective:**
BMI: 33.4 kg/m² (obesity range).
General: well-appearing, no acute distress. Post-bariatric surgery status with no reported complications.

**Assessment:**
45-year-old male, ~6–7 months post bariatric surgery, being screened for enrollment in an anti-obesity medication trial.
1. History of bariatric surgery — recovering well, no complications, appropriate weight loss trajectory with a common early plateau.
2. Obesity — BMI 33.4 kg/m², relevant to study eligibility criteria.
3. Nutritional maintenance — inconsistent protein and calcium intake; suboptimal hydration; mild orthostatic-type lightheadedness likely multifactorial (reduced intake volume, fluids).
4. Mild intermittent reflux with late meals.
5. Research eligibility — surgical history and current status to be reviewed carefully by study team; no disqualifying findings identified today, no enrollment decision made.

**Plan:**
- Provided detailed study information sheet for review at home; emphasized voluntary participation and right to withdraw at any time. No enrollment decision today.
- Reinforced protein intake goal of 60–80 g/day; encouraged strategies for busy days.
- Encouraged consistent calcium supplementation in addition to current multivitamin and B12.
- Advised increasing daily fluid intake to help with lightheadedness; rise slowly from seated/lying positions.
- Continue current walking regimen; supportive of gradual return to additional activity.
- Counseled to avoid late/large meals to reduce nighttime reflux.
- Patient to contact clinic if lightheadedness worsens or new symptoms arise before the next visit.
- Follow-up to discuss study participation after patient reviews materials.
after_visit_summary:
Thanks for coming in for your screening visit, David. This was a first step to see whether you might be a good fit for the anti-obesity medication study — no decisions were made today, and joining would be completely your choice.

How you're doing since surgery:
- You're about 6–7 months out from your bariatric surgery and recovering well, with no complications.
- Your weight is trending down (from about 250 lb to the low 230s). A plateau at this stage is normal — keep going.
- Your energy and sleep are much better, which is a great sign.

Things to work on before your next visit:
- Aim for 60–80 grams of protein daily, even on busy days.
- Take your calcium supplement consistently (keep up your multivitamin and B12 too).
- Drink more fluids through the day — this should help with the lightheadedness you mentioned. Stand up slowly.
- Keep up your evening walks.
- Avoid large or late meals to cut down on nighttime reflux.

Please read the study information sheet at home and bring any questions to your next visit. Call the clinic sooner if your lightheadedness gets worse or you notice any new symptoms.

metadata.visit_title: Research screening visit — anti-obesity medication study

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Priya. Come on in and have a seat. It's nice to finally meet you in person.

PT: Good morning. Thanks for fitting me in.

DR: Of course. So today is a screening visit for the research study we talked about on the phone — the one looking at a new anti-obesity medication. Before we get into any of the study details, I really just want to hear how you've been doing, especially since your surgery. Remind me, when was that?

PT: It was back in late July, so we're coming up on about seven months now.

DR: Seven months, great. And how has that whole journey been for you?

PT: Honestly, better than I expected. The first month was rough — I was really tired and figuring out what I could and couldn't eat — but after that it got a lot easier. I feel like a different person some days.

DR: That's wonderful to hear. Let's talk about weight for a second. Do you have a sense of how much you've lost since the surgery?

PT: I do, because I track it. I was around 205 pounds right before surgery, and this morning my scale said 168. So about thirty-seven pounds.

DR: That's a really solid trajectory for this point after surgery. Has the weight loss been steady, or has it kind of plateaued lately?

PT: It was fast at the beginning, like the first three months. Now it's slowed way down. The last month or so I've maybe lost two pounds. That's actually part of why I was interested in the study — I don't want to stall out.

DR: That makes sense, and that slowing is very typical around this stage. Your BMI today is 30.6, which puts you just into what we'd call the obesity range, so you're right at that threshold where people often start thinking about what comes next. Tell me about your eating now. What does a typical day look like?

PT: I eat small amounts, obviously. I do a protein shake in the morning because I can't really handle solid food that early. Lunch is usually chicken or fish and some vegetables. Dinner is small — sometimes I can only get through a few bites. I try to hit my protein goal but some days it's hard.

DR: Are you working with a dietitian?

PT: I was seeing one every month right after surgery. I've kind of dropped off — now it's more like every couple months.

DR: Okay. We can talk about reconnecting there, because protein and staying hydrated stay important long-term. Any trouble with foods not sitting well, nausea, dumping?

PT: A little dumping if I eat something too sweet. I learned that lesson early. Mostly I just avoid sugar now.

DR: Smart. How about your energy? You mentioned being tired early on.

PT: Energy's much better now. I actually have energy to do things after work, which I didn't before. I've started walking most evenings.

DR: That's fantastic. How much walking are we talking?

PT: Maybe thirty minutes, four or five days a week. Sometimes I'll do a longer walk on the weekend. I keep meaning to try strength training but I haven't yet.

DR: Walking is a great foundation, and adding some resistance work would help you hold onto muscle as you lose weight — that's something worth building toward. Any other concerns since surgery? Hair, dizziness, anything at all?

PT: My hair thinned out around month three, which freaked me out, but it seems to be coming back. No dizziness. I take my vitamins.

DR: The hair thinning is really common after this kind of surgery and it usually recovers, so that lines up. Good that you're on your vitamins. Any medications right now?

PT: No, nothing regular. Just the vitamins and a chewable calcium.

DR: Okay, good to know. Let me tell you about the study itself. It's looking at whether adding a weight-management medication after bariatric surgery helps people continue losing weight and keep it off, compared to placebo. If you enroll, you'd be randomly assigned to either the active medication or a placebo — meaning neither of us would know which you're getting during the study.

PT: So I might not get the real one?

DR: Correct, it's a possibility. There's typically a fifty-fifty chance. Everyone in the study still gets regular check-ins, weight monitoring, and dietary support, which some people find valuable on its own.

PT: That part sounds good actually. What would I have to do?

DR: There'd be regular visits — some for labs, weight, and questionnaires. The medication, if you're on the active arm, is a once-weekly injection you'd give yourself. We'd teach you how. There can be side effects like nausea, which sometimes overlaps with the GI sensitivities you already have from surgery, so we'd monitor that closely.

PT: I'm already used to injections from being diabetic-adjacent stuff my mom does, so needles don't scare me.

DR: Good. Today's visit is just screening — I'll take your history, we'll do some baseline measurements, and there'll be some paperwork and consent forms to review. You don't have to decide anything this minute. You can take the consent home and think it over.

PT: I appreciate that. I think I'm interested, but I'd like to read it.

DR: Absolutely, that's exactly the right approach. I'll have our coordinator go over the schedule with you and answer logistics questions. Do you have any questions for me right now?

PT: Just — if I'm losing weight fine on my own, is the medication overkill?

DR: That's a fair question. Many people do plateau after surgery, and the study is really about that next phase. Your current progress is great, and nothing about joining would take away the good work you're doing — it just adds monitoring and possibly the medication. And you can withdraw anytime.

PT: Okay. That helps.

DR: Wonderful. Let's get your measurements and I'll bring the coordinator in.
note:
**Subjective:**
Ms. Priya Salvatore is a 37-year-old woman presenting for a research screening visit for an anti-obesity medication study, approximately seven months status-post bariatric surgery (performed late July). She reports doing well overall since surgery. Pre-operative weight was approximately 205 lb; current self-reported weight is 168 lb, for a total loss of ~37 lb. Weight loss was rapid in the first three months and has since slowed, with only ~2 lb lost in the past month. She expresses interest in the study in part due to this expected plateau.

Diet: tolerating small volumes; morning protein shake, lunch of lean protein and vegetables, small dinners. Occasional dumping symptoms with sweets, which she now avoids. Working to meet protein goals. Previously seeing a dietitian monthly, now approximately every two months. Energy has improved significantly since the early post-op period. Activity: walking ~30 minutes, 4-5 days per week; interested in adding strength training but has not yet. Reports transient hair thinning around month three that appears to be recovering. Takes a multivitamin and chewable calcium. No dizziness. No regular prescription medications.

**Objective:**
BMI: 30.6 kg/m2.
Patient appears well, in no distress, engaged and appropriate throughout the visit.

**Assessment:**
37-year-old woman, ~7 months post bariatric surgery, with good post-operative weight loss now slowing as expected. Current BMI 30.6 kg/m2 (obesity range). Tolerating post-surgical diet with mild, avoidable dumping symptoms. Improving energy and consistent aerobic activity. Presenting for screening for an anti-obesity medication trial.

1. Obesity (BMI 30.6) — appropriate candidate for anti-obesity medication study given post-surgical weight plateau.
2. History of bariatric surgery — tolerating diet well; mild dumping with sweets, hair thinning recovering, on vitamin/calcium supplementation.

**Plan:**
- Reviewed study rationale, design (randomized, placebo-controlled), once-weekly self-administered injection for active arm, visit schedule, and potential GI side effects that may overlap with post-surgical sensitivities. Emphasized voluntary participation and right to withdraw.
- Provided informed consent documents to review at home; no enrollment decision required today.
- Baseline screening measurements obtained; study coordinator to review logistics and questionnaires.
- Encouraged reconnecting with dietitian for ongoing protein and hydration support.
- Encouraged gradual addition of resistance/strength training to preserve lean mass during continued weight loss.
- Continue multivitamin and calcium supplementation.
- Follow up per study protocol pending consent.
after_visit_summary:
Thanks for coming in today, Priya. This was a screening visit for the weight-management medication research study.

How you're doing: You're about 7 months after your bariatric surgery and doing well — you've lost about 37 pounds, your energy is much better, and you're walking most days. Your weight loss has slowed recently, which is normal at this stage.

What we talked about:
- Keeping up your protein and fluids, and reconnecting with your dietitian.
- Adding some strength/resistance exercise when you're ready.
- Continuing your multivitamin and calcium.

About the study: This trial looks at whether a once-weekly injectable weight-management medication helps after surgery. If you join, you'd be randomly assigned to either the medication or a placebo, and you'd have regular check-ins. Possible side effects include nausea.

Next steps:
- Take the consent forms home and read them over — there's no rush and no decision needed today.
- Our study coordinator will walk you through the visit schedule and answer any questions.
- You can join, decline, or withdraw later at any time.

Please reach out if you have questions before your next contact.

metadata.visit_title: Research screening visit — anti-obesity medication study

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Delacroix. It's nice to meet you. I'm Dr. Okafor, one of the physicians running the weight management medication study. Thanks for coming in today.

PT: Good morning. Happy to be here. And please, call me Robert.

DR: Robert it is. So today is really a screening visit. I want to hear how you've been doing, take a look at how things have gone since your surgery, and then I'll walk you through what the study involves so you can decide if it's something you want to be part of. Sound okay?

PT: That sounds great. I've got a lot of questions, honestly.

DR: Perfect, ask away as we go. Let me start with the big picture. You had your bariatric surgery — remind me, when was that?

PT: It was back at the end of July last year. So we're coming up on about seven months now.

DR: Seven months out. And how has the whole thing been for you, overall?

PT: Honestly, a mixed bag but mostly good. The first couple months were rough — figuring out how to eat again, small portions, not rushing. But I've kind of settled into a rhythm now. I feel a lot better than I did before.

DR: That's really good to hear. Let's talk about the weight trend. Do you have a sense of how much you've lost?

PT: Yeah, I've been tracking it. I was around 290 pounds right before surgery. I'm down to about 218 now. So somewhere in the range of 70 pounds.

DR: That's substantial — that's great progress. How has the pace been lately? Still coming down, or has it started to level off?

PT: That's actually one of my concerns. The first four or five months it just fell off. Now, the last month or so, it's basically stalled. I've been kind of stuck around this same number.

DR: That's a very common pattern, and it's actually one of the reasons studies like this exist — to look at whether adding a medication can help people keep progressing after that early plateau. So it's timely that you're here. Let me ask about your eating. What does a typical day of food look like for you now?

PT: Breakfast is usually a protein shake or some Greek yogurt. Lunch might be some chicken and vegetables, small portion. Dinner similar — I really can't eat much at once, so I've learned to eat slowly and stop when I'm full.

DR: Are you able to hit your protein goals? A lot of folks after surgery struggle to get enough in.

PT: Most days, yeah. My dietitian wanted me around 70 to 80 grams. Between the shakes and the meals I usually get there. Some days are harder.

DR: Good. Are you keeping up with your vitamins — the multivitamin, and whatever else they had you on?

PT: Yes, the chewable multivitamin, and a calcium one. I try to be consistent but I'll admit I miss a day here and there.

DR: That's honest, and pretty typical. Just do your best to stay regular with them. How about fluids — staying hydrated?

PT: I carry a water bottle everywhere now. That's a habit I actually stuck with.

DR: Excellent. What about activity? Are you moving more than you were?

PT: Definitely more. I walk most days, maybe 30 minutes. And I started doing some light strength stuff at home — resistance bands, that kind of thing. Nothing crazy.

DR: That's really the right approach, especially the strength work — it helps protect your muscle while you're losing. How's your energy overall?

PT: Way better than before surgery. I used to be exhausted by the afternoon. Now I've got more in the tank. Not perfect, but a big improvement.

DR: And how about mood, sleep — anything going on there?

PT: Sleep's decent. Mood's good. I feel more optimistic than I have in a long time, honestly. Losing the weight has helped my confidence.

PT: Can I ask — with the plateau, is that why you'd think a medication would help?

DR: Exactly right. The study is looking at an anti-obesity medication in people who've had bariatric surgery and want additional help reaching their goals. The idea is that surgery and medication can work together. Now let me explain how the study is structured. It runs about twelve months. There'd be regular visits here — roughly monthly at first, then spacing out. At each visit we check your weight, vitals, ask about how you're feeling, and do some bloodwork to keep an eye on your health.

PT: And the medication — is it a pill or an injection?

DR: In this study it's a once-weekly injection you'd give yourself at home, and we'd teach you exactly how. Some participants receive the active medication and some receive a placebo — that's part of how these trials work — and neither you nor I would know which during the study.

PT: Okay. Are there side effects I should worry about?

DR: The most common ones are gastrointestinal — nausea, sometimes some queasiness, especially early on. We start low and go slow to minimize that. We'd go through all of that in detail in the full consent document before you commit to anything.

PT: That makes sense. What happens today, then?

DR: Today is just screening. I've reviewed your history, we've talked through how you're doing, and I have your recent measurements — your BMI today is around 31, which fits the range we're looking at for the study. The next step is some screening labs and a review of the consent form. You take that home, read it, no pressure, and if you're interested we bring you back to enroll.

PT: I like that there's no pressure. I'm definitely interested.

DR: Wonderful. Do you have any other concerns before we wrap up the conversation part?

PT: I think just the plateau, which we covered. And I want to make sure I don't lose muscle.

DR: Both very reasonable. Keep up the protein and the resistance work — that's your best defense on the muscle side. I'll have the coordinator come in to go over the paperwork and get those screening labs. Thanks for being so thorough with me, Robert.

PT: Thank you, Doctor. This was helpful.
note:
**Subjective:**
Mr. Robert Delacroix is a 50-year-old man presenting for a research screening visit for an anti-obesity medication trial, approximately 7 months following bariatric surgery (performed late July 2025). He reports doing well overall since surgery. Initial post-operative months were challenging as he adjusted to reduced portion sizes and slower eating, but he has since settled into a stable routine and feels considerably better than pre-operatively.

Weight trend: reports pre-surgical weight of approximately 290 lb, now approximately 218 lb (~70 lb loss). He notes weight loss was rapid for the first 4-5 months but has plateaued over the past month, which is his primary concern.

Diet: typical intake includes a protein shake or Greek yogurt for breakfast, small portions of lean protein and vegetables at lunch and dinner. Reports meeting protein goal of 70-80 g/day most days. Takes chewable multivitamin and calcium supplement, with occasional missed days. Good hydration, carries water throughout the day.

Activity: walks ~30 minutes most days; performs light home resistance band training. Reports improved energy compared with pre-surgery, when he experienced afternoon fatigue. Sleep is adequate and mood is good; reports improved confidence and optimism.

Concerns: weight plateau and preservation of lean muscle mass. No GI complaints, no other acute concerns raised today.

**Objective:**
BMI: 31.2 kg/m2.
Patient appears well, in no distress, engaged and cooperative throughout the visit.

**Assessment:**
50-year-old man approximately 7 months status post bariatric surgery with successful weight loss (~70 lb) now plateauing over the past month. Current BMI 31.2 kg/m2, consistent with obesity and within the eligibility range for the anti-obesity medication study. He is nutritionally engaged, adherent to a high-protein diet and supplements with minor lapses, and physically active including resistance training. No acute concerns.

1. History of bariatric surgery — doing well, appropriate post-operative course, weight loss plateau noted.
2. Obesity, BMI 31.2 kg/m2 — active; candidate for study.
3. Interest in study participation — appropriate candidate, motivated.

**Plan:**
- Reviewed structure of the 12-month anti-obesity medication trial: monthly visits initially with later spacing, weekly self-administered subcutaneous injection, randomized placebo-controlled design, and monitoring of weight, vitals, and labs at visits.
- Reviewed common side effects (primarily GI, mitigated by slow titration); full details to be provided in informed consent document.
- Proceed with screening labs today.
- Provide informed consent document for patient to review at home; no pressure, will schedule enrollment visit if patient wishes to proceed.
- Counseled to continue high-protein intake (70-80 g/day) and resistance training to preserve lean muscle mass during ongoing weight loss.
- Encouraged consistency with multivitamin and calcium supplementation and continued hydration.
- Study coordinator to review paperwork and coordinate screening labs.
after_visit_summary:
Thank you for coming in today, Robert. This was a screening visit to see if the weight management medication study is a good fit for you.

How you're doing: You're about 7 months out from your bariatric surgery and have lost roughly 70 pounds — great progress! You mentioned your weight has leveled off in the past month, which is a very normal pattern and one of the reasons this study exists. You're eating well, hitting your protein goals most days, taking your vitamins, staying hydrated, walking most days, and doing some light strength exercises. Your energy and mood have improved.

What we talked about:
- The study lasts about 12 months, with visits that start monthly and then space out.
- It involves a once-weekly injection you would give yourself at home (we would teach you how). Some participants get the active medicine and some get a placebo, and neither you nor the doctor knows which.
- The most common side effects are stomach-related, and we lower the chance of these by starting with a low dose.

Keep doing:
- High-protein meals (aim for 70-80 grams per day) and your resistance-band exercises to help protect your muscle.
- Your multivitamin and calcium supplement daily.
- Plenty of water.

Next steps:
- We did some screening lab work today.
- Take the consent form home to read. There is no pressure. If you decide you'd like to join, we'll schedule a visit to enroll you.

If you have questions before your next visit, please reach out to the study coordinator.
